turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE ROBERTS
turns.text: Justice Jackson has the opinion of the Court this morning in case 23-1345, Rivers against Guerrero.

turns.name: JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: Incarcerated individuals are generally afforded one fair opportunity to challenge their imprisonment through a federal habeas petition. But as we have said before, when a prisoner seeks to bring a subsequent federal habeas challenge, the road gets rockier. That is because under the Antiterrorism and Effective Death Penalty Act of 1996 or AEDPA, second or successive habeas corpus applications face significant procedural hurdles. For example, such petitions cannot raise previously presented claims and they cannot present new claims without the filer having first obtained the Court of Appeals' authorization to file and meeting strict substantive requirements. Also, AEDPA's restrictions apply whether or not the prisoner calls the subsequent filing a habeas petition. Regardless of the label, if the new submission is a second or successive application, AEDPA's filing restrictions are applicable. This case presents the question of when AEDPA's second or successive restrictions kick in. Specifically, do those restrictions apply to a second in time habeas filing when the District Court has entered a judgment denying the first application on the merits, but that judgment is still under review on appeal? We hold today that in general, a second in time habeas petition qualifies as a second or successive application if the District Court has entered a judgment with respect to the first habeas petition regardless of its status on appeal. Petitioner Danny Rivers was convicted of various State Court offenses and incarcerated. In 2017, a Federal District Court denied Rivers' first habeas petition on the merits. He appealed that denial to the Fifth Circuit. While his appeal was pending, Rivers discovered new evidence and filed a second habeas petition presenting that evidence and new claims regarding it in the Federal District Court. The District Court classified that second in time habeas filing as a second or successive application under AEDPA and transferred it to the Fifth Circuit for authorization to file. But Rivers appealed the transfer arguing that the filing was not a second or successive petition, because his first petition remained on appeal. The Fifth Circuit affirmed the District Court's treatment of the second in time petition. In its view, entry of the judgment on the first petition was what marked the dividing line for second or successive treatment and the fact that the first petition was on appeal did not affect that characterization. The Fifth Circuit's decision entrenched a circuit split over how to characterize second in time habeas applications filed while an appeal is pending on the first application. We granted certiorari to resolve that split and as noted, we hold today that it is the entry of judgment not the conclusion of appellate review that demarcates second or successive applications. In reaching this conclusion, we reject the primary argument from Rivers' petition for certiorari, which frames this debate as being one about timing. He says that a second in time petition cannot be characterized as a second or successive application until the conclusion of appellate review of the first petition. Respondent disagrees. It asserts that a new petition filed after the District Court's judgment is entered with respect to the first petition qualifies as a second or successive application under AEDPA. We generally agree with respondent. Our case law compels the conclusion that judgment is the relevant event not the conclusion of appellate review. We have already explained, for example, that an amended petition filed after the initial application but before judgment on the first petition is not second or successive, because final judgment has not issued. On the other hand, a motion for relief from judgment under Federal Rule of Procedure 60(b) filed obviously after judgment has issued counts as a second or successive application if the filing, for example, seeks to add a new ground for relief not addressed by the initial judgment. Rivers points to a case in which we held that Rule 59(e) motions to alter or amend a judgment related to a first habeas petition are not second or successive even though they are filed after the judgment. But Rule 59(e) motions present a unique variant to the generally applicable rule and they do not qualify as a second or successive petition, because they suspend finality of the initial judgment and help to produce a single final judgment for appeal. Rivers' citations to history and policy also failed to persuade us that he has the better argument. By enacting AEDPA's restrictions, Congress chose to promote finality and avoid piecemeal litigation, which Rivers' view would undermine. Moreover, pre-AEDPA case law is inconclusive about when a subsequent habeas filing was dismissed as abuse of the writ rather than being considered on the merits and thus, is not dispositive. Undaunted Rivers' merits we think pivots to an alternative theory that second in time filings that specifically request amendment of an initial habeas petition do not by their nature qualify as applications subject to AEDPA's second or successive restrictions. But Rivers failed to present this argument in his petition for certiorari and did not raise it in the courts below. Moreover, his case does not present the factual predicates necessary to support this theory. Thus, we decline to address his alternative argument. For these reasons and others, we lay out in our opinion the judgement of the Fifth Circuit is affirmed. The Court's decision is unanimous.
